examples.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8-whitegrid')
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5), dpi=120)

months = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr']
revenue = [120, 135, 150, 145]

ax.set_title('Revenue trend by month', fontsize=14, weight='bold')

ax.set_ylabel('Revenue (k USD)')
ax.set_facecolor('#f7fbff')

commands.description: plt.close closes a figure to free memory in loops and batch jobs. Use it for configuring chart canvases and defaults and shipping stakeholder-ready reporting visuals. Key parameter: fig.

parameters.name: fig

examples.title: plt.close primary workflow
examples.scenario: plt.close used in a realistic business workflow with active command execution.
examples.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6,4), dpi=120)
ax.plot([1,2,3],[2,3,5], marker='o')
plt.close(fig=None)
plt.tight_layout()
print(type(ax).__name__)

overview: Matplotlib is the low-level charting engine for precise visualization control, custom annotations, and publication-quality figure export.
applications: Executive-ready KPI charts and trend diagnostics.
Fine-grained control over axes, legends, labels, and layout.
Batch generation and export of reporting visuals.
